content:
The goal for the I/O infrastructure is to generalize its API to be able to represent arbitrary backends. The first step in this direction has been done with [1].

This has the advantage that:

  1. We can build a common register abstraction (with the register!() macro [2]) on top of this generic interface, making it accessible for all busses and memory backed backends.

  2. We can share infrastructure that otherwise would be duplicated over and over again. For instance, dma_read!() and dma_write!() can be generalized over I/O backends with trait methods for read_field() and write_field().

The duplication we would have without (2) came up for iosys_map for instance [3]. With generic I/O backends we have a generalization that is much more powerful than things like iosys_map though.

As we have more people working on contributions towards those goals, let's use this thread for coordination.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260121202212.4438-1-zhiw@nvidia.com/
[2] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260129-register-v5-5-c4587c902514@nvidia.com/
[3] https://lore.kernel.org/all/DFOP5BY09539.AFY5L5FV1HNV@kernel.org/
Hmm, I'm not sure if DMA should really share the infra with I/O and registers. To me ultimately the dma_read!() dma_write!() is to be replaced with field projection.

It's the exact same concept, I/O memory and DMA memory is in the end just an abstract backing storage that is shared between a device and the CPU. Same goes for any other device memory, regardless of through which bus it is connected to the CPU.

I.e. for all of those the concept of registers, field accesses and "unsafe slices" is applicable.

Look at C infrastructure like iosys_map [1] which builds exactly this abstraction (of course without all the nice advantages we can get from Rust's type system for building such an abstraction).

[1] https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/latest/source/include/linux/iosys-map.h
For I/O memory you can't really create a slice unsafely, this will be some capability unique to DMA memory
You could still provide the abstract ability to memcpy from/to IO or DMA, though.
You can have some UnsafeSlice or IoSlice type that internally holds a pointer and provides you with the corresponding implementation for accessors such as memcpy_to, memcpy_from, etc.
This works for any backing storage, including DMA.

Sure, that's what I purposed in https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/DFZHE3PGMQHS.3F108QF6VQEQ7@garyguo.net/. However I imagine for DMA memory you would want the ability to touch these memory directly without having to copy.

Gary Guo said:

Hmm, I'm not sure if DMA should really share the infra with I/O and registers. To me ultimately the dma_read!() dma_write!() is to be replaced with field projection.

I guess we could share the same infra by supporting MMIO with projections, too.

It'll be of a bit different paradigm to the current register!() design, as you'll have a view of IO that is strongly typed (i.e. the I/O struct is typed itself, rather than having each register being typed)

Gary Guo said:

Sure, that's what I purposed in https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/DFZHE3PGMQHS.3F108QF6VQEQ7@garyguo.net/. However I imagine for DMA memory you would want the ability to touch these memory directly without having to copy.

Not sure that's exactly the same idea as what I proposed here (actually it probably is :slight_smile:): https://lore.kernel.org/all/DFSKLTVPNQ11.26N3WVT559F22@kernel.org/

But either way, you don't have to copy it, you can touch it directly, through register accesses (through the register!() macro, raw reads/writes (e.g. read32()), field accesses (equivalent to the current dma_read!() / dma_write!()) and through the IoSlice, which provides you accessors, such as memcpy_to_*. The latter can be compatible with anything, e.g. another IoSlice of a different type or UserSliceWriter / UserSliceReader.
This covers anything that you'd want to do with DMA memory. or any other (I/O) memory that is shared with a device.
Using read32() on DMA memory seems wrong to me.
Also what's the use case of making register!() work with DMA memory?

Boqun Feng said:

Using read32() on DMA memory seems wrong to me.

Or maybe the proposal is using a different function other then readl() to implement read32() for DMA?
I was mostly worried about using C's readl() on DMA memory.
but seems that's not the case given how iosys_map handles DMA vs MMIO?

Or maybe the proposal is using a different function other then readl() to implement read32() for DMA?

The backend implementation has to be specific for the backend. So, no this would not use readl().

Gary Guo said:

It'll be of a bit different paradigm to the current register!() design, as you'll have a view of IO that is strongly typed (i.e. the I/O struct is typed itself, rather than having each register being typed)

I did some experiment to see if the untyped Io design we have today can be made compatible with the strongly typed dma::CoherentAllocation design. This is indeed possible. Some snippets:

/// Trait to represent compile-time known size information.
trait KnownSize {
    /// Minimum size of object of this type.
    const MIN_SIZE: usize;

    /// Maximum size of object of this type.
    fn maxsize(p: *const Self) -> usize;
}

// For sized types, everything is known.
impl<T> KnownSize for T {
    const MIN_SIZE: usize = core::mem::size_of::<T>();

    fn maxsize(_: *const Self) -> usize {
        Self::MIN_SIZE
    }
}

impl<T> KnownSize for [T] {
    const MIN_SIZE: usize = 0;

    fn maxsize(p: *const Self) -> usize {
        p.len() * core::mem::size_of::<T>()
    }
}

#[repr(transparent)]
struct SliceLargerThan<const SIZE: usize, T>([T]);

impl<const SIZE: usize, T> KnownSize for SliceLargerThan<SIZE, T> {
    const MIN_SIZE: usize = SIZE * core::mem::size_of::<T>();

    fn maxsize(p: *const Self) -> usize {
        (p as *const [T]).len() * core::mem::size_of::<T>()
    }
}

struct TypedMmio<T>(*const T); // aka __iomem T*

type Mmio<const SIZE: usize> = TypedMmio<SliceLargerThan<SIZE, u8>>;

I have a question about the design of generalising I/O backends. I noticed that in the CoherentAllocation code, it has safety requirements such as the caller must ensure that there are no races with concurrent reads+writes. For generalising over all I/O backends, is the plan to take the union of all safety pre-conditions for all backends and have that in the interface?
The safety precondition is for getting a slice, not dma_read/dma_write

Hey, I got paged into this thread from https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260203-qcom-socinfo-v2-2-d6719db85637@google.com/ , where I was trying to add methods to copy slices or arrays in/out of IO via memcpy_toio and memcpy_fromio.

Questions:

  1. Are there patches in progress that aren't linked here already?
  2. When it comes to typed IO, are we considering the possibility of types which may be larger than the underlying region (the socinfo region from the series I'm working on) or which are DSTs (the versions regions in the series I'm working on)
  3. What differentiates an IoSlice from an object implementing the Io trait + an offset/length? Anything?
  4. If we want IoSlice to be a concrete type rather than a trait (implied above, but not explicitly stated?), then getting access to the underlying Io is either going to mean we have IoSlice<T> where T: Io, which complicates interfaces, or we're going to end up with dyn Io everywhere.
  5. Is IoSlice intended to be an owning type or a borrow?

I think there is no work in progress yet, but I think @Eliot Courtney and @Gary Guo are looking into this as well.

The main idea of IoSlice would be to have something that deals with the fact that we can't take a "real" slice of I/O memory, DMA memory, etc. You could also say UnsafeSlice. I think it should be a borrow of the I/O backend and provide methods that forward to the I/O backend implementation depending on what source or destination we are copying from / to.

So, let's say you have a dma::CoherentAllocation<T>, then we could have a method as_slice() (or as_io_slice()) that returns a IoSlice<'io, T>. IoSlice<T> could have a method copy_from_slice()that takes a "normal" Rust slice.

Similarly, you could also have methods that copy from a UserSlice or interact with UserSliceReader and UserSliceWriter directly. We could also have methods that allow us to copy back and forth between two IoSlice<T> with different I/O backends.

This way users never have to think about what exact lower level accessors we need to copy from or to certain memory, as everything is abstracted in I/O backends and IoSlice.

Given the apparent need to have a non-array CoherentAllocation (actually, looks like DmaGspMem is already using slice with fixed size of 1), I think the final result would be more like a IoView rathan IoSlice. So take Nova code for example:

#[repr(C)]
struct Msgq {
    tx: MsgqTxHeader,
    rx: MsgqRxHeader,
    msgq: MsgqData,
}

#[repr(C)]
struct GspMem {
    ptes: PteArray<{ GSP_PAGE_SIZE / size_of::<u64>() }>,
    cpuq: Msgq,
    gspq: Msgq,
}

we should have ability to project &'a dma::CoherentAllocation<GspMem> into something like io::View<'a, dma::CoherentAllocation<GspMem>, Msgq> and nestly project into io::View<'a, dma::CoherentAllocation<GspMem>, MsgqTxHeader> (and so on).

Slice use case would really just be a small part of this, so you can a sub-slice projection from dma::CoherentAllocation<[Foo]> into io::View<'a, dma::CoherentAllocation<[Foo]>, [Foo]>. And going from slice to element is just an index projection.

The dma_read!/dma_write! macro today is already doing projection, just always a index projection plus a field projection, where I see no reason that this cannot be generalized.

The exact API and surface syntax is something to be figured out, but I think it'll be something on this line.
Interesting, if I understand your proposal correctly all the stuff I described would still work, plus it would cover the projection use cases.
Yes, this is more generic, type IoSlice<'io, T> = IoView<'io, [T]>
Seems like a good idea, but it also looks pretty complicated.
Not really, it only looks complicated because I throw in CoherentAllocation<T> into the view type which I think is needed anyway as you need to way to specify the backend.
Given Eliot's series https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20260130-coherent-array-v1-0-bcd672dacc70@nvidia.com/ about adding CoherentArray/CoherentObject this is probably an simplification, even.
Yeah, I think we can also build it up incrementally, e.g. we could start with only implementing the methods for copying to/from "normal" slices for IoView<'io, [T]> to satisfy Matt's use-case.

Gary Guo said:

Given Eliot's series https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20260130-coherent-array-v1-0-bcd672dacc70@nvidia.com/ about adding CoherentArray/CoherentObject this is probably an simplification, even.

Agreed.
I've started the work and will be pushing WIP to here: https://github.com/nbdd0121/linux/commits/io_projection/. So far I've done some improvement of CoherentAllocation and nothing generic yet.
Looks like those changes cover a bunch of what we discussed AFAICT. Gary, is there anything left for me to do w.r.t. my original patch series or have you got this covered?

@Danilo Krummrich would you be ok if I added a backend backed by system memory? We communicate with the firmware by reading and writing from a large region of shared memory, it would be cool if we could use something like the register!() macro to describe the layout and then use the generic I/O layers for reads and writes. I suspect that we might introduce some repetition otherwise (probably using core::mem::[read|write]_volatile(), which is undesirable
cc @Boris Brezillon fyi
is this normal shared memory, not dma coherent allocation?
This is normal shared memory, yes
actually, it depends what you mean by dma coherent. The allocation itself goes through shmem, but mapping might be uncached
but we don't have dedicated accessors to access those memory regions, it's just regular loads/stores to pointers
Do you not even require volatility or atomicity?

Eliot Courtney said:

Looks like those changes cover a bunch of what we discussed AFAICT. Gary, is there anything left for me to do w.r.t. my original patch series or have you got this covered?

I think everything would be covered. Thank you for doing the work, it is very valuable in providing use cases to shape the design of new API.

Daniel Almeida said:

Danilo Krummrich would you be ok if I added a backend backed by system memory?

I think that's perfectly fine as long as it is specific for the requirements of the shared memory, i.e. as Gary mentions volatility and atomicity requirements.

Do you not even require volatility or atomicity?

Locking and mem barriers are going to be handled at a higher level, because it only has to happen on specific registers

The flow is:

  • update reg X, Y, Z
  • make sure mem accesses are flushed
  • ring a doorbell which is an actual IoReg
as for the locking, ideally we'll lock entire portions of memory to guarantee atomicity of the update, and that will be done with a lock at a higher level
I don't think the IoBackend needs to be involved, at least not in our case
When you mention locking, I assume that you think about tearing. None of the I/O backends prevents tearing for concurrent accesses. So, here the expectation is always that drviers take care of this.
Maybe that implies having a custom backend, dunno
Looking at the flow it is not too different from some Nova GSP code is doing. I think you can still use dma coherent memory?
you mean the backend for coherent memory?
Yes, the dma::CoherentAllocation that we have today. Does this memory region need to be obtained via other means?
it does
As I said, it's memory allocated through shmem
the allocation itself can't be done through dma_alloc_coherent()
Ah, missed that part. So I think you probably need something that is allocated differently, but access-wise is equal to dma::CoherentAllocation.
How bad would it be to implement a custom IoBackend?
I'll consider this when desigining the generic I/O API

@Gary Guo Boris is likely referring to [1].

[1] https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v6.18.6/source/drivers/gpu/drm/drm_gem_shmem_helper.c#L52
Okay, thanks.
(@Boris Brezillon Please correct me if that's wrong.)

Boris is likely referring to [1].

That's the gem frontend for shmem-based backing, yep, but the actual page allocation happens in mm/shmem.c
what's important is that it's regular system memory
the CPU mapping we access can be cached or uncached, but it's all done with regular load/store instructions anyway
So this is something that's userspace created via memfd and shared with the driver, like dmabuf?
I assume it's the same for dma_coherent memory

So this is something that's userspace created via memfd and shared with the driver, like dmabuf?

Nope, we have our own shmem_fs mount point behind drm_device objects
I think the same impl would work for both dma coherent and this, the worst case we just duplicate the impl. But to your original question, yes this is doable.
Perfect!

Nope, we have our own shmem_fs mount point behind drm_device objects

That, or we use the default shmem_fs mountpoint, when the driver doesn't instantiate a custom one, but it's all happening internally, no need for the user to pass a memfd or a dmabuf

@Gary Guo

Do you not even require volatility or atomicity?

Not sure if this was your question, but we need volatile reads and writes, yes, since the memory accesses will have side effects

Not sure if this was your question, but we need volatile reads and writes, yes, since the memory accesses will have side effects

We can do with regular accesses, I think (actually, we do it in C), because the synchronization is done explicitly (when we ring the doorbell). For instance, any writes to the config regs will be flushed by the memory barrier that exists when we write to the register. For reads, it's not a problem is those are elided when the result of the read is not read, because, again, it's not IO, the other end (the FW) doesn't see whether the value has been read or not, and ordering, we can enforce it with acual memory barriers (the the ringbell barrier is not enough).

That's not to say volatile accesses won't work (and I actually think we should go for volative accesses if the backend exists), but they're not strictly needed most of the time.
IO trait uses &self for access, so if volatile is to be omitted, it needs to be !Sync
Or atomic
I see

Hey Gary, I checked your code for digging the IoView ideas. It seems there has been KnwoSize for comile-time known size. What's the next step for IoView ideas? I am mostly wondering how to cope my PCI capability patches with your ideas. :)
I plan to add io::View<'a, Base, T> where Base is a type that implements Io and T is a type with KnownSize bound

Alice Ryhl said:

Or atomic

Because kernel atomics are volatile?
Because having multiple concurrent reads/writes is safe if they are atomic.

Alice Ryhl said:

Because having multiple concurrent reads/writes is safe if they are atomic.

How is &self: Sync with volatile operations OK?
For MMIO they're okay.

Even per-reg atomicity wouldn't be enough for our use case anyway, so we'll have locks to protect updates on top. Not to say we don't want that guarantee at the IoBackend, but Tyr won't need it for its FW <-> CPU interfaces.

I see. For capabilities, Io::View would store the offset. And fixed-size caps would be fine. How about the variable-length caps e.g. Vendor-specific VNDR?

Also curious how io::View will handle field projection, since I/O
accesses can't use pointer derefs like DMA does, will it be something else?

Gary Guo said:

I plan to add io::View<'a, Base, T> where Base is a type that implements Io and T is a type with KnownSize bound
projection can start with arbitrary pointer without provenance and then only use the offset.
Would like to rebase my patches on your brach asap when it is ready. :)
It might take a while as I'm currently on holiday

Gary Guo said:

I've started the work and will be pushing WIP to here: https://github.com/nbdd0121/linux/commits/io_projection/. So far I've done some improvement of CoherentAllocation and nothing generic yet.

Just pushed my latest WIP progress. Mostly there, a lot of tidy up and comment writing still needs to follow. https://github.com/nbdd0121/linux/commit/491a1cfb4102341adc7623b0abab6718d1876959 is the nova-core driver being converted to the new API
Nice to see these horrible transmute methods in fwres.rs going away!

Hi folks. I re-worked my previous implementation of PCI capability support on top of this infrastructure. It was posted as RFC here.

https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20260225180449.1813833-1-zhiw@nvidia.com/T/#t
I/O projection on the list: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20260323153807.1360705-1-gary@kernel.org/
Cool!! Let me rebase my vGPU staging tree and PCI capability patches on top of it.

I noted that rustfmt will format

project!(foo, [variant ..end])

to

project!(foo, [variant..end])

Would this change our conclusion on putting variant name inside [] for index projection? @Benno Lossin @Alice Ryhl @Danilo Krummrich @Miguel Ojeda
ah because we allow subslicing...

Some ideas:

  • there is an unstable option to disable this behavior (sadly unstable....)
  • we could make people use project! { ... } since that doesn't format (also not really nice, since people might just use normal parens)
  • we could change the , to a => and then it probably won't format it like that anymore, right? (this is probably my current favorite)
Or we just say this is fine :)

project!(foo, [variant..end])

Ick! I think we should look for an alternative syntax, something along the lines of Benno's 3rd point above. But I'm not sure about the arrow.

Gary Guo said:

Or we just say this is fine :)

I don't want that :)
Can we just add a colon after the variant?

Danilo Krummrich said:

But I'm not sure about the arrow.

The only other alternatives that are allowed to be after an expression is the ;

another option would be to add a sigil after the keyword, so:

  • project!(foo, [variant! ..end])
  • project!(foo, [variant: ..end])
project!(foo, [variant; ..end]) perhaps?
I like semicolon
If we can't have the colon, I prefer semicolon over the arrow.
Good thing about semicolon over colon is that rustfmt is able to format the index expression
comma is okay, too
Does rustfmt work with !?
Nope
Actually with the try variant rustfmt cannot format any of the proposals, because try is a lang keyword

Gary Guo said:

project!(foo, [variant; ..end]) perhaps?

oh I actually meant:
project!(foo; [variant ..end])

Danilo Krummrich said:

If we can't have the colon, I prefer semicolon over the arrow.

the colon inside of the [] should work
I think I also like that one best

• project!(foo => [variant ..end])
  • project!(foo, [variant: ..end])
I want something generally formatable
My general preference would be project!(foo, [variant: ..end]), but if we can have something that can be processed by rustfmt making a compromise seems reasonable.
I'm adding this as a discussion topic tomorrow

Gary Guo said:

I want something generally formatable

oh yeah that'd be good

I mean the original [variant idx] version also wasn't formattable...

I know we tried to avoid nesting with another layer, but we could have project!(foo, [variant!(..end)])
Actually the field projection syntax with leading dot is also not formatable. So colon is fine
but we could also not have a leading dot?
I put it there for extensibility, so we could add other projection types, e.g. custom projection that is just an expression. I mostly have the register macro in mind when designing the syntax

I can tell you from experience that formattability is pretty important for a macro, I think it's one of the "mistakes" we made when designing init!. It's of course a tradeoff, since <- makes it clear when an initializer is used
maybe it's not as bad for the project macro, since there won't be "big expressions" put in there, but who knows...
for project macro the mut keyword also blocks formatting.
Perhaps the long term solution is to have custom formatting plugin for rustfmt so macros can be customize formatted (just thinking out loud)
Yeah, I think for project! it is probably fine, as the expressions won't get crazy, for init! is a much bigger concern.

Sashiko pointed out issues with alignment of untyped regions in https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260608-io_projection-v3-0-c5cde13a5ec4%40garyguo.net, which after some thinking, I think is a more general problem.
I did a quick grep on device trees, there are GPIO drivers which use 1-byte-aligned (or sized) I/O regions too, and I would expect them to want to use the same API that requests IoMem
Perhaps it's inevitable that we add custom alignment support for I/O regions? @Alexandre Courbot
Does this mean the content might also be accessed unaligned? I.e. what if you have an I/O region of [u32] with an alignment of 1?
Misaligned access is still broken at least for MMIO, so this should be forbidden by io_addr_assert
So if you requested a region with alignment of 1, then you can only use read u8 with non-fallible methods, try_ would do a runtime alignment check.
But is that a possible access pattern based on what you have seen? Or are all the 1-aligned I/O regions accessing [u8]s?
well if it's C I guess they are all [u8] by definition, but you probably see what I mean. :)
They're just doing byte access
There's no atomicity for non-aligned access anyway, so if a driver needs to do misaligned access they'd better just read multiple times :)
My point here is that we cannot just say "untyped region has a fixed alignment" and satisfy all drivers

@Danilo Krummrich given this is kinda existing issue, how should I handle this for I/O projection series? I'm inclined to say "Region" has 4-byte alignment (and add checks to constructor) so Tyr and Nova are happy, and we can extend it with customizable alignment later on?
Would the alignment become a generic parameter then? I see you are using #[repr(align(x))]so I'm not sure what we can do concretely (sorry, still need to go in depth through the series)
You can do this: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/DILXW5AK9O8E.3TESJB45UPTZM@garyguo.net/

I already have a working version of Region type that just adds another parameter

#[repr(C)]
pub struct Region<const SIZE: usize = 0, const ALIGN: usize = 4>
where
    Align<ALIGN>: ValidAlign,
{
    align: Align<ALIGN>,
    inner: [u8],
}

but this also needs to be propagated to Bar and IoMem to which point I think it's becoming big enough change that it might deserve its own series and not just fixed inside the projection series :)
(Well, probably not Bar, as I think bars are sufficiently aligned always)
if this can be done as a follow-up then I think I would be grateful. The current series already packs quite a few punches. :)
but yeah if alignment can be an optional generic parameter then it would only become visible when actually needed, so I think I like that.
Most pointer types imply alignment by having a target type
Which is why I'm trying to have Region to have the correct alignment, it's the "Target" type for untyped regions.

Yes, the current code assumes that the base address is aligned according to the access primitives that are used, which should be sufficient.

You correctly identified the the gap with qword accesses, which should be fixed by considering the base address, which your patches already do.
Well, the issue is that the base Region type, if made 4-byte aligned, is too restrictive

An example is arch/arm/boot/dts/cirrus/ep7209.dtsi with GPIO address of 0x80000001 and size 1. drivers/gpio/gpio-clps711x.c uses devm_platform_ioremap_resource to map it. If we make Region 4-byte aligned, then our Rust map API needs to reject it
So for this case, it needs to be able to switch to a 1-byte aligned region (or have API available to just do map and get IoMem<u8> perhaps?)
Yes, I'm well aware, I wasn't done writing (currently on the run). :)
Everything else, i.e. Region being generic over the base alignment can be done subsequently, or rather when it is needed.

I think it is a very uncommon use case and the example you found is already a bit odd, as it creates a driver instance per GPIO bank. If it wouldn't and just handle all banks of the controller in a single driver instance, it would be 4 bytes aligned.
(I found the same example after reading your post.)
